exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy and Reliability
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI outputs are trustworthy, accurate, and consistent with clinical reality.

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI systems generating incorrect alerts or missing real pathology.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: High false positive rate in incidental findings
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: AI flags benign variants (e.g., lung nodules <4mm) as suspicious, leading to unnecessary follow-up.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Missed subtle pathologies
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: AI fails to detect early-stage cancers or subtle fractures that experienced radiologists would catch through pattern recognition.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Over-reliance on training data biases
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: AI performs poorly on underrepresented populations or rare diseases not well captured in training sets.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Explainability and Trust
exp_L2_00_01.description: Difficulty understanding why an AI made a specific recommendation, undermining confidence.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Black-box decision logic
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: AI provides no interpretable rationale for its findings, making validation difficult during time-sensitive reads.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Heatmaps highlight irrelevant areas while ignoring clinically meaningful regions, creating cognitive dissonance.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Inconsistent performance across modalities
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: AI excels on CT but underperforms on MRI or ultrasound due to differing image physics and artifacts.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration and Efficiency
exp_L1_01.description: Challenges in embedding AI tools into existing clinical reading environments without disrupting productivity.

exp_L2_01_00.label: PACS/RIS Compatibility
exp_L2_01_00.description: Technical and ergonomic mismatches between AI outputs and radiology information systems.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Poor integration with legacy PACS
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: AI results appear in separate windows or require manual import, breaking reading flow.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Non-standardized result formats
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: Each AI vendor uses different annotation styles or measurement units, increasing cognitive load.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Disruption of hanging protocols
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: AI overlays interfere with custom window/level presets or series ordering preferences.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Time and Cognitive Load
exp_L2_01_01.description: AI adds steps or decisions that increase rather than reduce workload.

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Need to verify every AI suggestion
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Radiologists feel compelled to double-check all AI outputs, negating time savings.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Alert fatigue from low-specificity tools
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: Constant pop-ups for non-actionable findings desensitize users to critical alerts.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: Uncertainty about when to override AI
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: Ambiguity in borderline cases creates decision paralysis and slows reporting.

exp_L1_02.label: Clinical Responsibility and Liability
exp_L1_02.description: Uncertainty about accountability when AI contributes to diagnostic errors.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Medicolegal Ambiguity
exp_L2_02_00.description: Lack of clear guidelines on who is liable—radiologist, hospital, or AI vendor—when AI errs.

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: No legal precedent for AI-assisted errors
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: Courts haven't established standards for negligence involving AI recommendations.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Fear of being second-guessed by AI in court
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: Concern that a jury might believe the AI over human judgment, even if the AI was wrong.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Audit and Documentation Burden
exp_L2_02_01.description: Increased need to document interactions with AI for compliance and quality assurance.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Mandatory logging of AI overrides
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Regulatory requirements force time-consuming notes justifying why AI suggestions were rejected.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Inconsistent audit trails across platforms
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: Different AI systems record usage differently, complicating departmental QA reviews.

exp_L1_03.label: Data and Model Limitations
exp_L1_03.description: Fundamental constraints in how AI models are trained and validated relative to real-world imaging diversity.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Generalizability Across Populations
exp_L2_03_00.description: AI models trained on narrow datasets fail in diverse clinical settings.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Poor performance on non-Western or pediatric cohorts
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: Models trained predominantly on adult Caucasian populations misinterpret anatomy or pathology in other groups.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Failure on atypical presentations
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: AI misses diseases that manifest unusually (e.g., pneumonia without consolidation), which humans recognize via contextual clues.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Image Quality and Artifact Sensitivity
exp_L2_03_01.description: AI degrades significantly with suboptimal scans common in real practice.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Misinterpretation of motion or metal artifacts
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: AI confuses streak artifacts for pathology (e.g., calling metal streaks 'hemorrhage').

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Inability to adjust for protocol variations
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: Same anatomy scanned with different kVp/mAs or slice thickness confuses the model.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: Overfitting to idealized academic images
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: Models trained on pristine research-grade images fail on noisy, truncated, or off-center real-world studies.

exp_L1_04.label: Professional Identity and Autonomy
exp_L1_04.description: Concerns about AI diminishing the role, expertise, or judgment of radiologists.

exp_L2_04_00.label: Deskilling and Over-reliance
exp_L2_04_00.description: Fear that junior radiologists will lose critical diagnostic skills by depending on AI.

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: Trainees bypassing foundational learning
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: Residents accept AI conclusions without developing independent pattern recognition abilities.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Gradual erosion of differential diagnosis reasoning
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: AI’s single-label outputs discourage consideration of alternative diagnoses.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Value Perception by Referrers
exp_L2_04_01.description: Referring physicians may undervalue radiologist input if they believe AI is doing the 'real work.'

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: Misattribution of diagnostic credit
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: Clinicians thank the 'AI system' rather than the interpreting radiologist, affecting professional recognition.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: Pressure to adopt AI without evidence
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: Administrators mandate AI use based on marketing claims, not clinical validation, undermining professional autonomy.

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability in Clinical Context
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how radiologists interpret and use AI-generated explanations during diagnosis.

res_L2_00_00.label: Visual Explanation Utility
res_L2_00_00.description: How visual cues like heatmaps support or hinder diagnostic reasoning.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify whether clinicians prioritize AI heatmaps, raw imaging features, prior cases, or other clinical data when triaging AI alerts.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Assess clinician interpretation of heatmap as model reasoning
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore whether clinicians conflate heatmap overlays with the model’s actual decision logic or treat them as heuristic aids.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Evaluate impact of heatmap color scheme on perceived trustworthiness
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate whether perceptual properties (e.g., red vs. blue) influence trust independent of diagnostic content.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explanation Calibration and Accuracy Perception
res_L2_00_01.description: How clinicians understand and respond to model calibration and performance nuances.

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Clarify clinician understanding of 'calibrated' AI models
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Determine if clinicians equate calibration with general accuracy or recognize it as reliability across confidence levels.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Identify thresholds where small confidence changes alter diagnostic behavior
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Map sensitivity to minor confidence score differences (e.g., 0.62 vs. 0.58) to understand decision boundaries.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Assess consistency of interpretation near chance-level confidence (e.g., 0.52–0.55)
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Explore whether clinicians treat scores just above 0.5 as noise, signals, or require additional verification.

res_L1_01.label: AI Integration into Diagnostic Workflow
res_L1_01.description: How AI recommendations are incorporated—or ignored—within real-world clinical routines.

res_L2_01_00.label: Workflow Interruption and Override Points
res_L2_01_00.description: Situations where clinicians deliberately disregard AI output due to workflow constraints or expertise.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Pinpoint specific phases (e.g., triage, final sign-off) where clinicians override or bypass AI suggestions.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Uncover coordination issues (e.g., between technologists, residents, attending radiologists) that degrade AI recommendation relevance.

res_L2_01_01.label: Decision-Making in Ambiguous Cases
res_L2_01_01.description: Strategies clinicians use when AI outputs are uncertain or conflict with human judgment.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Elicit step-by-step reasoning used to resolve uncertainty when AI and clinical intuition diverge.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Understand role of confidence scores in ambiguity resolution
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Determine how numerical confidence values factor into decisions when imaging findings are equivocal.

res_L1_02.label: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
res_L1_02.description: Expert-derived principles for safe, effective AI adoption in high-stakes diagnosis.

res_L2_02_00.label: Expert-Defined Safety Rules
res_L2_02_00.description: Core guidelines radiologists would teach novices about using AI responsibly.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Elicit a single safety rule for new radiologists using AI
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Capture distilled wisdom on avoiding overreliance, misinterpretation, or automation complacency.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Reveal unspoken beliefs about when AI fails or should not be trusted, even if not explicitly stated.

res_L2_02_01.label: Trust Calibration and Interface Design
res_L2_02_01.description: How interface elements shape appropriate levels of trust in AI outputs.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Assess whether visual design choices (e.g., color) unduly influence trust
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine if aesthetic or perceptual features override clinical content in shaping confidence in AI.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Explore desired features for communicating model uncertainty
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Infer from expert responses what forms of uncertainty representation (beyond scalar scores) would support better decisions.

aligned_path_expert: High false positive rate in incidental findings
aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.expert_branch: High false positive rate in incidental findings
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on reducing unnecessary follow-ups caused by high false positives in incidental findings—like benign lung nodules—is critically important for patient safety and efficient resource use.

pivot.limitation: However, even if AI accuracy improves, clinicians may still override or ignore its alerts at key decision points, undermining the intended clinical benefit.
pivot.research_goal: Identify stages in the diagnostic workflow where AI is most likely ignored or overridden by clinicians.
pivot.compelling_reason: By understanding exactly when and why AI suggestions are bypassed, we can design interventions that preserve your gains in diagnostic precision and ensure those improvements actually translate into real-world practice.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, radiologists receive AI alerts only at moments they trust and act on them—like during initial triage—not during rushed sign-offs. As a result, fewer benign findings trigger unnecessary callbacks, and patients avoid anxiety and extra scans, while clinicians feel supported rather than burdened.

aligned_path_researcher: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

conflict.researcher_branch: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

probes.question: When reviewing an image where AI missed a subtle finding, is your concern more related to unfamiliar visual texture patterns or deviations in expected contrast?
Which matters more when you suspect a missed pathology: whether the finding aligns with known anatomic relationships or whether it matches historical case patterns?
Is your ability to detect early-stage cancers more dependent on recognizing subtle contrast deviations or on evaluating spatial context around the region?
choices: Evaluating spatial context around the region

probes.question: When assessing a potential subtle fracture, do you prioritize local edge features or the broader spatial arrangement of surrounding structures?
choices: Broader spatial arrangement of surrounding structures

probes.question: Which contributes more to catching missed pathologies: comparing current images to prior studies or analyzing the current image in isolation?
choices: Comparing current images to prior studies
Analyzing the current image in isolation

probes.question: When distinguishing true pathology from artifact, is your judgment more influenced by your personal noise tolerance threshold or by lesion morphology cues?
In detecting subtle fractures, which is more decisive: disruption of expected edge continuity or deviation from normal tissue texture?
When evaluating a potential early tumor, do you rely more on intuitive lesion shape patterns or on quantitative contrast measurements?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the spatial context around a lung nodule were rearranged to mimic a benign configuration while keeping the nodule’s intrinsic texture unchanged, would your confidence in detecting it as malignant decrease?
If the imaging noise level increased just enough to obscure micro-calcifications but not affect larger structures, would your ability to identify early breast cancer drop significantly?

If a subtle rib fracture showed perfect edge discontinuity but occurred in an anatomically implausible location (e.g., mid-diaphysis without trauma history), would you still consider it a true positive?

aligned_path_expert: Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention
aligned_path_researcher: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

conflict.expert_branch: Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

attributes: Lesion conspicuity relative to background tissue

probes.question: Is your concern more about the AI heatmap disrupting your overall visual impression of the case, or about the heatmap missing a specific focal abnormality?
Which matters more when evaluating AI saliency maps: how sharply the highlighted edges align with true pathologic boundaries, or how broadly the map covers the suspicious area?
choices: Sharp alignment with true pathologic boundaries

probes.question: Do you rely more on AI highlighting that respects anatomic neighborhoods (e.g., lung vs. mediastinum), or on pixel-level intensity patterns regardless of location?
choices: Pixel-level intensity patterns regardless of location

probes.question: When assessing AI alerts, is it more critical that the saliency map aligns with changes seen since the last scan, or that it highlights abnormalities in the current scan alone?
choices: Alignment with interval changes from prior scans
Highlighting abnormalities in current scan alone

probes.question: Which causes greater cognitive dissonance: AI highlighting a region that is anatomically implausible for disease, or one that is plausible but clinically insignificant?
Does an AI heatmap that overemphasizes benign findings reduce your diagnostic confidence more than one that underemphasizes subtle malignancies?
Is your workflow more disrupted when AI draws attention away from your usual initial search zones, or when it confirms your initial focus but with poor boundary definition?
choices: Attention drawn away from usual initial search zones
Confirmation of focus with poor boundary definition

probes.attribute: Lesion conspicuity relative to background tissue
probes.question: Which factor better predicts whether you trust an AI saliency map: the contrast between lesion and surrounding tissue in the raw image, or the heatmap’s intensity gradient?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI saliency map consistently highlighted regions that respected known anatomic boundaries (e.g., confined to a lung lobe) instead of spilling across fissures, would your judgment about 'Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention' improve?

If the AI heatmap emphasized areas with high temporal change compared to prior imaging rather than static abnormalities, would your judgment about 'Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention' change?

If the AI’s highlighted edges matched the radiologist’s perceived pathologic margins within 2mm tolerance instead of being diffuse, would your judgment about 'Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention' improve?

aligned_path_expert: Fear of being second-guessed by AI in court
aligned_path_researcher: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
Assess whether visual design choices (e.g., color) unduly influence trust

conflict.expert_branch: Fear of being second-guessed by AI in court
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess whether visual design choices (e.g., color) unduly influence trust

analogy.source_concept: No legal precedent for AI-assisted errors

structural_mapping.inputs: An AI system provides a diagnostic recommendation that differs from the clinician’s judgment
structural_mapping.logic: In the absence of clear standards, courts may default to perceived objectivity—whether from algorithmic authority (expert concern) or interface cues like color (researcher concern)—to assign blame or credibility
structural_mapping.outputs: A post-hoc determination of fault that hinges not on clinical accuracy but on how trust was shaped by external, non-medical signals

analogy.explanation: Just as the lack of legal precedent creates uncertainty about how courts will weigh AI recommendations against human judgment, the researcher’s focus on visual design examines how superficial cues—like color—can unintentionally create a false sense of AI reliability. Both situations involve non-clinical factors (legal ambiguity or interface aesthetics) distorting the evaluation of medical decisions after the fact.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist uses an AI tool that highlights a lung nodule in bright red on a chest X-ray, suggesting malignancy; the radiologist, based on experience and additional context, interprets it as benign and documents this reasoning
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would test whether the red highlighting increased the radiologist’s (or a jury’s) trust in the AI’s conclusion—even if the underlying algorithm had low confidence—by comparing decisions made with red vs. neutral-colored overlays
scenario.edge_cases: If the AI was wrong but displayed its output in a high-trust visual format (e.g., red = urgent), a jury might defer to the AI despite the clinician’s correct judgment, conflating interface-induced confidence with clinical validity—precisely the medicolegal fear the expert describes, but triggered by a design choice the researcher studies

aligned_path_researcher: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

conflict.researcher_branch: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

probes.question: When AI misses an atypical presentation, is your concern more about the absence of prior diagnostic history or the lack of recent symptom evolution in the AI's analysis?
Which matters more when overriding AI output: local disease prevalence or patient travel/exposure history?
Is your suspicion of an atypical disease driven more by lab abnormalities in unrelated organ systems or by concurrent non-respiratory symptoms?
choices: Lab abnormalities in unrelated organ systems

probes.question: Do you rely more on the rate of symptom progression or the sequence of symptom onset to detect atypical presentations?
When evaluating AI disagreement, which carries more weight: chronic comorbidities or acute behavioral risk factors (e.g., substance use)?
Is inconsistency between reported symptoms and vital signs more concerning than mismatched symptom severity and functional status?
choices: Inconsistency between symptoms and vital signs
Mismatched symptom severity and functional status

probes.question: Does deviation from a patient’s usual health baseline matter more than absolute abnormality thresholds in identifying atypical disease?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If a patient with no known immunosuppression presented with patchy infiltrates but normal inflammatory markers, would your confidence in rejecting AI's 'no pneumonia' call decrease compared to if they had HIV?

If two patients had identical chest imaging but one’s symptoms worsened over 48 hours while the other’s were stable for 2 weeks, would you be more likely to suspect an atypical infection in the rapidly progressing case?

If a patient’s current presentation deviates significantly from their documented baseline (e.g., mild dyspnea now vs. none ever) but all vitals are within population-normal ranges, would you still override an AI that says 'low concern'?

aligned_path_expert: Inability to adjust for protocol variations
aligned_path_researcher: Clarify clinician understanding of 'calibrated' AI models

conflict.expert_branch: Inability to adjust for protocol variations
conflict.researcher_branch: Clarify clinician understanding of 'calibrated' AI models

structural_mapping.inputs: AI receives real-world scans that differ from training data in subtle but clinically common ways (e.g., noise, truncation, off-centering)
structural_mapping.logic: Model applies rigid decision boundaries learned from clean, standardized datasets without adapting to distributional shifts
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous or overconfident predictions because the model lacks mechanisms to recognize or compensate for input-domain mismatch

analogy.explanation: Just as a model overfits to pristine academic images and fails when confronted with real-world variability, a 'calibrated' AI model may appear reliable on benchmark data but mislead clinicians if its confidence scores don’t reflect true uncertainty under protocol variations. In both cases, the model’s internal logic doesn’t account for expected clinical heterogeneity—whether in image quality or probabilistic reliability—leading to misplaced trust.

scenario.inputs: A chest CT scan acquired at 100 kVp and 1.5 mm slice thickness (non-standard protocol) showing a small nodule; the same patient later scanned at 120 kVp and 3 mm slices (standard protocol). The AI model outputs a malignancy probability of 0.92 with 95% confidence on the first scan and 0.45 with 80% confidence on the second.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would evaluate whether the clinician interprets the 95% confidence as ‘this prediction is likely correct’ (accuracy) versus ‘when the model says 95% confidence, it is right 95% of the time across many similar cases’ (calibration). It would then assess if the clinician adjusts trust based on this distinction, especially given the protocol shift.
scenario.edge_cases: When the AI gives high confidence on a non-standard protocol scan (e.g., thin slices, low dose) that mimics artifact patterns from training data, the expert sees a failure of robustness to acquisition parameters, while the researcher sees a potential miscalibration—highlighting that the clinician might conflate consistent high confidence with correctness, even when the input violates the model’s operational assumptions.

aligned_path_researcher: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring clinicians recognize the radiologist’s interpretive expertise—rather than crediting the AI—is essential for maintaining professional identity and interdisciplinary respect in diagnostic workflows.

pivot.limitation: However, if unspoken assumptions about when AI fails remain hidden, misattribution may persist not just from oversight, but because referrers implicitly trust AI outputs in contexts where human judgment is actually critical.
pivot.research_goal: Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations
pivot.compelling_reason: By uncovering these hidden beliefs, you gain leverage to proactively shape how your expertise is positioned—not just as a corrective to AI, but as the indispensable anchor in high-stakes decisions, reinforcing both recognition and clinical authority.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day after this insight is integrated, referrers consult radiologists earlier in complex cases, explicitly referencing their judgment over the AI’s output. Radiologists are invited into multidisciplinary discussions not as AI validators, but as trusted diagnostic leads whose expertise defines safe, effective care.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

analogy.source_concept: High false positive rate in incidental findings

structural_mapping.inputs: AI receives imaging data containing benign or ambiguous features (e.g., small lung nodules) that resemble pathology in training data
structural_mapping.logic: AI applies pattern-matching rules derived from biased or overgeneralized training examples, triggering alerts even when clinical context suggests low risk
structural_mapping.outputs: Unnecessary follow-up recommendations that clinicians often disregard after applying their own judgment

analogy.explanation: Just as high false positives lead clinicians to ignore AI alerts during interpretation due to poor signal-to-noise ratio, the researcher’s focus on 'stages where AI is ignored' examines when and why this override behavior becomes systematic—particularly in workflow phases like final sign-off, where clinicians have already learned to distrust AI outputs shaped by unrepresentative training data.

scenario.inputs: A chest X-ray from a 28-year-old non-smoker showing a 3mm lung nodule; the AI system was trained predominantly on data from older smokers with confirmed malignancies
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s method would identify that at the 'final radiologist sign-off' stage, the clinician consistently overrides the AI’s 'suspicious nodule' flag, documenting it as a benign finding based on patient history and size criteria
scenario.edge_cases: When the same AI flags a subtle rib fracture in a trauma patient—a rare presentation underrepresented in training data—the clinician may also override it, but this time due to missed pathology rather than false alarm, revealing how both false positives and false negatives contribute to workflow bypass at different stages

aligned_path_researcher: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

conflict.researcher_branch: Determine what clinicians examine first when AI flags an abnormality

analogy.source_concept: Mismatch between AI saliency maps and radiologist attention

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated abnormality alert paired with a modality-specific image (e.g., MRI or ultrasound) and its associated saliency map
structural_mapping.logic: Clinician evaluates the alert by comparing the AI’s highlighted region against their own visual search pattern and clinical priors, much like they would assess whether a saliency map aligns with known pathology patterns
structural_mapping.outputs: A decision about whether to trust or override the AI based on alignment between what the AI emphasizes and what the clinician expects to see given the imaging modality and clinical context

analogy.explanation: Just as the expert is concerned that saliency maps may mislead by highlighting irrelevant areas—especially when image physics differ across modalities—the researcher’s focus on what clinicians examine first reveals how trust is modulated by this alignment. In both cases, the core issue is whether the AI’s visual rationale matches human perceptual expectations shaped by modality-specific expertise.

scenario.inputs: An abdominal ultrasound scan showing a hypoechoic liver lesion flagged by AI as suspicious for malignancy, accompanied by a heatmap overlay, patient history of cirrhosis, and a prior CT from six months ago showing stable benign cysts
scenario.outputs: Eye-tracking data and clinician interviews revealing that the radiologist first checks the prior CT comparison before inspecting the ultrasound heatmap, and only engages with the AI explanation if the lesion appears new or inconsistent with prior imaging
scenario.edge_cases: When the AI performs well on CT but poorly on ultrasound (due to speckle noise), the clinician may disregard the ultrasound heatmap entirely—even if the underlying pathology is real—because their mental model of AI reliability is anchored to CT performance, creating a false negative due to modality-based distrust rather than true diagnostic inaccuracy

aligned_path_researcher: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated diagnostic annotations arriving from external systems into the radiologist’s reading environment
structural_mapping.logic: The system fails to embed results natively, forcing the user to context-switch or manually reconcile data
structural_mapping.outputs: Fragmented workflow where critical AI insights are either missed, delayed, or require extra cognitive effort to interpret

analogy.explanation: Just as poor PACS integration forces radiologists to toggle between windows—breaking visual and cognitive continuity—the handoff problems the researcher studies involve discontinuities in human coordination (e.g., between technologist and resident) that similarly fracture the flow of AI-derived insight. In both cases, the AI output exists but isn’t seamlessly absorbed into the diagnostic chain, reducing its clinical utility.

scenario.inputs: A chest X-ray is analyzed by an AI tool that flags a 12mm nodule with volume measurements in cubic millimeters; the result is sent to a resident who uses a dictation template expecting diameter in centimeters, while the attending radiologist reviews prior studies annotated in RECIST criteria (longest diameter only).
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would map how this mismatch in measurement conventions and role-based expectations causes the AI finding to be downgraded, re-measured manually, or omitted from the final report—not due to AI inaccuracy, but because the handoff between roles lacked shared semantic protocols.
scenario.edge_cases: When the AI correctly identifies a subtle finding but the resident, trained on different annotation norms, dismisses it as artifact because the format doesn’t match departmental precedent—highlighting that the failure occurs not in AI performance but in cross-role interpretation alignment, which the expert’s focus on format standardization alone wouldn’t resolve.

aligned_path_researcher: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

conflict.researcher_branch: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

probes.question: Is your resistance to AI overlays more strongly tied to how long you've used your current hanging protocol or to how many custom presets it contains?
When AI disrupts series ordering, which matters more: preserving chronological alignment with prior studies or maintaining anatomical sequence within the current exam?
Does disruption occur more when AI overlays obscure your primary diagnostic zone or when they shift your secondary reference zones?
Do you reject AI overlays more often when your own diagnostic confidence is high or when the AI's stated confidence is low?
Is the disruption worse when AI forces a manual reset during initial read or during peer review sign-out?
Which causes more disruption: semi-transparent AI annotations that blend into the image or opaque bounding boxes that block underlying anatomy?
Are you more likely to accept AI interference in straightforward cases or in complex, multi-pathology cases?
Does your acceptance of AI disruption increase more when your immediate team uses it routinely or when department-wide protocols mandate it?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI overlay opacity decreased from 70% to 30%, would your judgment about 'Disruption of hanging protocols' change?
If the case shifted from a routine follow-up to a trauma with multiple acute findings, would your judgment about 'Disruption of hanging protocols' change?
If your personalized hanging protocol had been in use for only 1 week instead of 2 years, would your judgment about 'Disruption of hanging protocols' change?

aligned_path_expert: Need to verify every AI suggestion
aligned_path_researcher: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

conflict.expert_branch: Need to verify every AI suggestion
conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

analogy.source_concept: Uncertainty about when to override AI

structural_mapping.inputs: An AI-generated finding that falls in a diagnostic gray zone—neither clearly benign nor obviously pathological
structural_mapping.logic: The radiologist must decide whether to accept, reject, or modify the AI output without clear guidelines, leading to hesitation and extra verification steps
structural_mapping.outputs: Delayed report finalization and increased cognitive burden due to unresolved ambiguity

analogy.explanation: Just as uncertainty about when to override AI creates decision paralysis by leaving the radiologist without clear criteria for action, handoff problems reduce AI utility by introducing ambiguity about who is responsible for acting on or validating the AI’s output during transitions between team members (e.g., technologist to resident). In both cases, the core issue is a breakdown in role clarity that forces redundant verification, undermining efficiency.

scenario.inputs: A chest X-ray is acquired by a technologist; an AI flags a subtle nodule. The study is first reviewed by a resident who accepts the AI finding, but the attending radiologist—unaware the resident already validated it—re-runs the AI tool and re-evaluates the same nodule independently before signing off.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would identify this as a coordination failure at the handoff point between resident and attending: duplicated effort because the workflow lacks a shared signal indicating that the AI suggestion was already verified, thus reducing net time savings.
scenario.edge_cases: If the AI finding were high-confidence and critical (e.g., a large pneumothorax), both models might agree verification is necessary—but the expert sees this as inherent to AI use, while the researcher sees it as a symptom of poor handoff design that fails to distinguish urgent vs. routine validations across roles.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

analogy.source_concept: Uncertainty about when to override AI

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated alerts or suggestions that fall in a diagnostic gray zone (e.g., indeterminate lung nodule size)
structural_mapping.logic: Clinician must weigh trust in AI against clinical judgment, often without clear decision rules, leading to hesitation or inconsistent behavior
structural_mapping.outputs: Delayed decisions, variable override patterns, or inconsistent adherence to AI guidance

analogy.explanation: Just as uncertainty about when to override AI creates hesitation and inconsistent responses to borderline cases, the researcher’s focus on identifying workflow stages where AI is ignored reveals systematic patterns in those same moments of doubt—mapping not just the cognitive tension but the precise procedural points where it manifests as bypass or dismissal.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist reviewing a chest X-ray during triage receives an AI alert flagging a 4.2mm pulmonary nodule with low specificity (i.e., high false-positive rate based on prior data). The same AI system later flags a different finding during final sign-off—a subtle consolidation possibly indicating early pneumonia.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s method would log that the first alert was dismissed during triage (a high-override stage due to alert fatigue), while the second was acted upon during sign-off (a low-override stage where attention is heightened), thereby identifying triage as a critical point of AI disengagement.
scenario.edge_cases: If the radiologist overrides the AI during sign-off despite high confidence in the tool—perhaps due to a recent bad experience with a false negative—the expert might attribute this to generalized distrust, while the researcher would classify it as an anomaly at a typically low-override stage, prompting investigation into contextual factors like case complexity or user state.

aligned_path_expert: Uncertainty about when to override AI
aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.expert_branch: Uncertainty about when to override AI
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

attributes: Institutional policy clarity on AI use

probes.question: When deciding whether to override AI, does your concern stem more from personal risk tolerance or institutional risk guidelines?
Which matters more in your override decision: the time-critical nature of the case or the stability of the patient’s condition?
Is your hesitation to rely on AI driven more by fear of malpractice claims or by concerns about audit/documentation standards?
Do you trust the AI’s stated confidence score more when it aligns with your intuition or when it contradicts clinical norms?
choices: When it aligns with your intuition

probes.question: Which influences your current override behavior more: a past instance where overriding AI led to a good outcome or one where it led to a bad outcome?
When uncertain about overriding AI, do you prioritize seeking input from a senior colleague or deferring to department-wide precedent?
Does high comorbidity burden make you more likely to trust AI or more likely to override it?

probes.attribute: Institutional policy clarity on AI use
probes.question: Is your uncertainty about overriding AI greater when policies are ambiguous or when no formal policy exists at all?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI’s confidence score increased from 75% to 95%, would your judgment about 'Uncertainty about when to override AI' change?
If a recent override you made resulted in a near-miss event instead of a successful outcome, would your judgment about 'Uncertainty about when to override AI' change?
If the patient had three major comorbidities instead of none, would your judgment about 'Uncertainty about when to override AI' change?

aligned_path_expert: No legal precedent for AI-assisted errors
aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.expert_branch: No legal precedent for AI-assisted errors
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

analogy.source_concept: Fear of being second-guessed by AI in court

structural_mapping.inputs: Clinician receives an AI diagnostic suggestion that conflicts with their professional judgment
structural_mapping.logic: Clinician weighs the risk of legal scrutiny if they override the AI, anticipating that a future jury might defer to the AI’s output regardless of its accuracy
structural_mapping.outputs: Decision to either follow or ignore the AI, influenced by perceived legal vulnerability rather than purely clinical reasoning

analogy.explanation: Just as clinicians may alter their behavior due to fear of being second-guessed by AI in court—even when the AI is wrong—the researcher’s focus on identifying where AI is ignored reveals the exact moments this legal anxiety manifests in practice. Both concepts hinge on the clinician’s anticipation of post-hoc evaluation: one frames it as a medicolegal risk, the other maps its behavioral footprint in the diagnostic workflow.

scenario.inputs: An emergency department physician reviewing a chest X-ray flagged by an AI system as 'high probability of pneumothorax' during triage, but the physician—based on patient stability and clear lung sounds—believes it’s a false positive
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s method would log this as a 'triage-stage override,' categorize the reason (e.g., 'conflicting physical exam'), and correlate it with similar cases to identify patterns of AI dismissal at early workflow stages
scenario.edge_cases: If the patient later deteriorates and develops a pneumothorax, the expert’s concern about 'no legal precedent' becomes acute: was the override negligent? But the researcher’s data might show that overrides at triage are common and often clinically justified—highlighting a gap between legal uncertainty and observed workflow norms

aligned_path_researcher: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify implicit assumptions about AI limitations

structural_mapping.inputs: Multiple AI systems generating override decisions without standardized justification formats
structural_mapping.logic: Each platform applies its own implicit rules about what constitutes a valid reason to reject AI advice, leading to non-comparable records
structural_mapping.outputs: Fragmented, non-uniform documentation that hinders cross-platform quality assurance and regulatory review

analogy.explanation: Just as inconsistent audit trails reveal hidden differences in how platforms encode override justifications, identifying implicit assumptions about AI limitations exposes the unspoken, system-specific beliefs clinicians hold about when AI is unreliable—both cases surface unstated variability that undermines standardization and safety.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist using two different AI tools for lung nodule detection: Tool A flags a benign calcified granuloma as malignant; Tool B misses a subtle spiculated nodule. The radiologist overrides both but logs only 'clinical judgment' per hospital policy.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would extract the radiologist’s implicit assumptions—e.g., 'Tool A overcalls calcifications' and 'Tool B underperforms on spiculated margins'—even though these weren’t documented, enabling targeted model improvements or user training.
scenario.edge_cases: When an override is logged as 'AI not trusted today' without clinical specifics, the expert sees compliance fulfilled, while the researcher sees a lost opportunity to uncover context-dependent failure modes (e.g., fatigue, unusual anatomy) that could inform safer AI deployment.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

structural_mapping.inputs: Clinician decisions to reject or bypass an AI recommendation during a diagnostic task
structural_mapping.logic: System captures the override event and requires structured justification for audit purposes, linking action to accountability
structural_mapping.outputs: A standardized, traceable record that can be reviewed during QA or regulatory audits

analogy.explanation: Just as mandatory logging of AI overrides creates a structured trail tied to clinician actions for auditability, identifying stages where AI is most likely ignored reveals patterns in *when* and *why* those override events occur. Both concepts treat clinician deviation from AI as a signal: one focuses on documenting each instance for compliance, while the other analyzes the workflow context to predict or explain where such deviations are most frequent.

scenario.inputs: In a radiology department using two different AI tools—one for chest X-ray triage (Vendor A) and another for CT pulmonary embolism detection (Vendor B)—a senior radiologist consistently skips AI annotations during final sign-off but engages with AI during initial triage.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s method would produce a workflow map showing that final sign-off is a high-override phase across both platforms, even though Vendor A logs detailed override reasons while Vendor B only timestamps user dismissal without context.
scenario.edge_cases: If Vendor B’s system doesn’t log any override data at all, the expert sees a broken audit trail (inconsistent documentation), while the researcher still infers high ignore rates at sign-off via screen-tracking or session logs—highlighting a gap where audit incompleteness masks behavioral patterns the researcher seeks to uncover.

aligned_path_expert: Poor performance on non-Western or pediatric cohorts
aligned_path_researcher: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

conflict.expert_branch: Poor performance on non-Western or pediatric cohorts
conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

structural_mapping.inputs: Unusual or non-standard clinical manifestations (e.g., pneumonia without consolidation) that deviate from the training data distribution
structural_mapping.logic: AI relies on pattern matching from common cases and fails to integrate contextual or inferential cues that human clinicians use to recognize deviations
structural_mapping.outputs: Missed or incorrect diagnoses due to rigid adherence to learned patterns rather than adaptive reasoning

analogy.explanation: Just as AI fails on atypical disease presentations because it lacks the contextual flexibility to interpret deviations from its training norms, it also fails during handoffs in clinical workflows when coordination gaps cause inputs to shift unexpectedly—such as a resident overriding an AI flag without documenting rationale, or a technologist using a different imaging protocol. In both cases, the AI’s output becomes unreliable not because of internal flaws alone, but because the real-world input context diverges from the assumptions embedded in its training or deployment design.

scenario.inputs: A pediatric chest X-ray from a rural clinic in India is uploaded to an AI diagnostic system trained primarily on adult Caucasian patients from U.S. academic hospitals; simultaneously, the ordering physician is a general practitioner, the interpreting radiologist is a senior attending working remotely, and the image was acquired on an older machine with lower resolution.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a workflow map identifying specific handoff points—e.g., between technologist and GP, GP and radiologist, or radiologist and AI interface—where communication breakdowns (like missing metadata about patient age or scanner type) cause the AI’s recommendation to be ignored, misinterpreted, or applied incorrectly, even if the model’s raw output were technically usable.
scenario.edge_cases: The AI correctly flags a mediastinal shift consistent with congenital heart disease in a 2-year-old, but the attending radiologist dismisses it because the AI’s confidence score appears low (due to poor calibration on pediatric data), and no handoff protocol exists to flag that this case involves a non-Western pediatric patient—thus conflating data bias (expert’s concern) with workflow fragility (researcher’s concern).

aligned_path_expert: Misinterpretation of motion or metal artifacts
aligned_path_researcher: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
Elicit a single safety rule for new radiologists using AI

conflict.expert_branch: Misinterpretation of motion or metal artifacts
conflict.researcher_branch: Elicit a single safety rule for new radiologists using AI

structural_mapping.inputs: Real-world clinical scans with noise, truncation, or suboptimal positioning (as opposed to clean research datasets)
structural_mapping.logic: AI model applies decision rules learned from pristine data that don’t generalize to messy reality, leading to false positives or missed findings
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous diagnostic labels due to mismatch between training conditions and deployment context

analogy.explanation: Just as models overfit to idealized academic images fail when confronted with real-world imperfections, a safety rule for new radiologists must acknowledge that AI outputs are only reliable within the narrow conditions of their training. The researcher’s goal—to distill a single safety rule—mirrors the expert’s concern: both recognize that AI misinterprets artifacts not because it’s 'wrong' in isolation, but because it lacks contextual awareness of how real-world imaging deviates from its training distribution. The safety rule serves as a human-level analog to dataset curation: it constrains usage to contexts where the model’s logic remains valid.

scenario.inputs: A non-contrast head CT from the ER showing dense streaks from dental fillings across the basal ganglia; the AI flags a hyperdense region as 'acute hemorrhage'
scenario.outputs: The elicited safety rule would be: 'Always verify AI-detected hemorrhage in regions prone to metal artifact by checking raw slices, windowing, or prior imaging before acting.'
scenario.edge_cases: When motion artifact mimics subtle subarachnoid blood in a trauma patient with no metal, the AI may still hallucinate pathology—but the safety rule focused on metal might not trigger, revealing a gap between artifact-specific heuristics and broader overreliance risks the researcher aims to address.

aligned_path_researcher: Safety and Heuristics for AI Use
Elicit a single safety rule for new radiologists using AI

conflict.researcher_branch: Elicit a single safety rule for new radiologists using AI

analogy.source_concept: Misinterpretation of motion or metal artifacts

structural_mapping.inputs: Real-world clinical images containing non-pathological artifacts (e.g., metal streaks, motion blur) that deviate from clean training data
structural_mapping.logic: AI model applies pattern recognition trained on idealized images, misclassifying artifact features as diagnostic signals due to lack of robustness to real-world variability
structural_mapping.outputs: False-positive findings (e.g., labeling metal streaks as hemorrhage), leading to incorrect clinical conclusions

analogy.explanation: Just as AI misinterprets artifacts because it was trained only on pristine images, a safety rule for new radiologists must guard against overreliance on AI outputs that appear confident but are based on inputs the model wasn't adequately trained to handle. The safety rule functions like an artifact-awareness protocol: it doesn’t fix the model’s flaw but instructs the human to question AI outputs when image quality or acquisition conditions deviate from ideal—mirroring how radiologists learn to distinguish true pathology from imaging artifacts.

scenario.inputs: A portable chest X-ray taken in an ICU with patient motion, low contrast, and a central line artifact, fed into an AI pneumonia detector trained on high-quality PA erect chest X-rays from outpatient clinics
scenario.outputs: The elicited safety rule: 'Always verify AI findings against image quality and acquisition context before acting—especially if the study is non-standard, truncated, or contains hardware.'
scenario.edge_cases: The AI confidently flags 'consolidation' in a blurry, rotated supine X-ray, but the finding is actually due to poor inspiration and beam hardening. The expert sees this as a data limitation issue; the researcher treats it as a failure of human-AI interaction requiring a behavioral guardrail, not a model retraining problem.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify stages in workflow where AI is most likely ignored

probes.question: Is your concern more about trainees not asking 'What if?' questions or not seeking alternative diagnoses when AI provides a clear answer?
Which matters more: trainees seeing fewer rare cases due to AI filtering, or trainees not mentally rehearsing visual/textual patterns after AI gives a label?
choices: Fewer rare cases due to AI filtering
Not mentally rehearsing patterns after AI labeling

probes.question: Is the bigger issue trainees being overconfident when AI agrees with them, or underconfident when AI disagrees?
Do you observe more offloading during initial impression formation or during final diagnostic justification?
Is feedback degradation worse when AI is correct but trainees don’t understand why, or when AI is wrong but trainees don’t notice?
At what point do trainees stop engaging critically: when cases are moderately complex or only when they’re highly complex?
Does bypassing foundational learning increase more under tight shift deadlines or during high patient volume surges?
Is the risk of skill atrophy higher when AI gives no explanation or when it gives a plausible but incorrect explanation?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI system started providing saliency maps that highlight key image features for every prediction, would your judgment about 'Trainees bypassing foundational learning' change?
If trainees were required to document one alternative diagnosis before accepting the AI’s top suggestion, would your judgment about 'Trainees bypassing foundational learning' change?
If the average case complexity in the training set decreased by 30% due to AI pre-filtering out ambiguous cases, would your judgment about 'Trainees bypassing foundational learning' change?

aligned_path_expert: Gradual erosion of differential diagnosis reasoning
aligned_path_researcher: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

conflict.expert_branch: Gradual erosion of differential diagnosis reasoning
conflict.researcher_branch: Characterize handoff problems that reduce AI utility

probes.question: When reviewing an AI suggestion, do you prefer systems that list multiple plausible diagnoses or a single top diagnosis?
Which matters more in your diagnostic process: clear AI certainty scores or the ability to see low-probability alternatives?
Is your concern about AI outputs stronger when the case is used for teaching residents or when it’s a routine clinical case?
Are you more likely to override an AI recommendation if it presents high confidence or low confidence?
Does the erosion of differential reasoning bother you more in straightforward cases or in diagnostically ambiguous cases?
Which impacts your willingness to consider alternatives more: time pressure or cognitive load from multitasking?
Is your reliance on AI’s single-label output higher when you expect agreement with colleagues or when you anticipate disagreement?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the AI system changed from showing only the top diagnosis to displaying the top three ranked differentials with probabilities, would your judgment about 'Gradual erosion of differential diagnosis reasoning' change?

aligned_path_expert: Pressure to adopt AI without evidence
aligned_path_researcher: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

conflict.expert_branch: Pressure to adopt AI without evidence
conflict.researcher_branch: Document clinician process for accepting/rejecting AI in ambiguous cases

structural_mapping.inputs: An AI system provides a diagnostic suggestion that aligns with or diverges from the clinician’s judgment
structural_mapping.logic: The clinician must decide whether to accept, modify, or reject the AI input while navigating external pressures and internal professional standards
structural_mapping.outputs: Attribution of diagnostic responsibility—either to the clinician (preserving autonomy) or to the AI (eroding professional identity)

analogy.explanation: Just as misattribution of diagnostic credit shifts recognition away from the clinician toward the AI—even when the clinician did the interpretive work—the process of documenting how clinicians accept or reject AI in ambiguous cases reveals where professional judgment is exercised versus where it is overridden. Both concepts hinge on preserving the visibility and integrity of clinician reasoning under conditions where AI presence distorts accountability and autonomy.

scenario.inputs: A radiologist reviewing a lung nodule CT scan receives an AI alert flagging a 6mm nodule as 'high risk for malignancy' (based on a non-validated algorithm), but their clinical experience and comparison with prior imaging suggest stability consistent with a benign granuloma.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a structured record of the radiologist’s step-by-step reasoning: e.g., '1. Compared current nodule morphology to 12-month prior; 2. Noted calcification pattern consistent with histoplasmosis; 3. Cross-referenced patient travel history; 4. Overruled AI due to longitudinal evidence contradicting malignancy likelihood.'
scenario.edge_cases: When hospital policy mandates logging AI recommendations as part of the official report—even if rejected—the radiologist may feel compelled to include the AI output without critique, conflating documentation with endorsement. This creates tension: the researcher seeks transparent reasoning, but the expert experiences this as institutional pressure that undermines autonomy, especially when the AI tool lacks clinical validation.
